Private networking is a tenant convention: there are 56 private endpoints and 74 private DNS zones indicating frequent use of Private Link, so plan the finance web app and its Azure SQL backend to use Private Endpoints and integrate with the tenant private DNS zones to keep traffic on the VNet.

Managed identity preference is clear: many resources (storage, container registries, web sites) use user-assigned or combined identities (storage accounts show 55% UserAssigned), so provision a user-assigned managed identity for the web app and grant it least-privilege access to the database and Key Vault for secrets access.

Key Vault posture is strict: vaults uniformly enable purge protection and long soft-delete (90 days) with network ACLs commonly configured, so store DB credentials in Key Vault with purge protection enabled and restrict vault access to the finance app's VNet or private endpoints.

SQL network exposure is inconsistent: 57% of SQL servers allow public network access while 43% have it disabled, so for an internal finance app choose a SQL deployment with public network access disabled and a private endpoint to avoid internet exposure.

Strong TLS baseline exists: multiple services (Storage, Event Hubs, Cognitive Services) enforce TLS 1.2 by default, so require TLS 1.2+ on the web application and database connections to align with tenant security expectations.

Perimeter controls are commonly used: tenant resources include WAF-capable front doors and Application Gateway WAF_v2 instances, so for any externally accessible interfaces consider an internal Application Gateway (or restrict Front Door) with WAF rules tuned for finance workloads and block direct app access.

Cost/size patterns favor mid-tier SKUs but also Hyperscale for databases: many app plans are Basic/B1 and 60% of SQL databases show Hyperscale HS_Gen5, so pick a database SKU aligned to expected size—use Hyperscale if growth/large dataset is expected, otherwise choose a GeneralPurpose tier to match common cost-conscious app plans.

Backup and recovery are institutionalized: 90 recovery services vaults typically have infrastructure encryption enabled, so include automated backups and retention in the plan and register database backups with a Recovery Services vault (or equivalent managed backup) with encryption enabled.
